summary:
The Chalukya Dynasty — particularly the Badami Chalukyas (543–753 AD) and later the Chalukyas of Vengi and Kalyani — was one of the dominant powers of the Deccan plateau in medieval South India, ruling from their capital at Badami (modern Karnataka) and competing with the [[event:2173|Pallavas]] of Kanchipuram and the [[event:2172|Rashtrakutas]] in the endless wars for Deccan dominance. The Badami Chalukyas reached their peak under Pulakesi II (reigned c. 610–642 AD), who famously halted the northward expansion of Emperor Harsha of Kannauj at the Battle of the Narmada River — one of the few major military defeats Harsha suffered — and established the Chalukyas as the foremost power in southern India.

The Chalukya period produced remarkable artistic achievements, particularly in rock-cut cave temple architecture. The cave temples at Badami, carved into red sandstone cliffs, contain masterpieces of early medieval Hindu, Jain, and Buddhist iconography; the Virupaksha Temple at Pattadakal (a UNESCO World Heritage Site) represents a synthesis of northern Nagara and southern Dravida architectural styles that influenced temple construction across South Asia. The Chalukyas also left important administrative legacies, maintaining the Deccan as a distinct political zone between the northern Indian empires and the southernmost Tamil kingdoms. The dynasty declined in the mid-8th century under Rashtrakuta pressure, but related Chalukya branches continued to rule in Vengi (eastern Deccan) and later at Kalyani until the 12th century.
